title: W7 D Suggested Exercises
body:
Suggested Exercises for Week 7 Juan Carlos Carbajal

ECON 3202 — Term 1, 2026 UNSW Sydney

Problem 1. Suppose the function g : R2 → R satisfies the following property:

| g( x1 , x2 )| ≤ x12 + x22 ,

for all ( x1 , x2 ) ∈ R2 . Show that g admits partial derivatives with respect to both x1 and x2 at x̂ = ( x̂1 , x̂2 ) = (0, 0).

Problem 2. Let u : R n+ → R be a Cobb-Douglas utility function, i.e.,

u( x) = x1α1 x2α2 · · · xnαn ,

where αi > 0 for all i = 1, . . . , n.1 In a classic microeconomics problem, a consumer is endowed with a Cobb-Douglas utility function and maximizes her utility subject to her budget constraint, for given prices p = ( p1 , . . . , pn ) > 0. Since consuming zero amount of any good leaves the consumer with zero utility, and consuming any small amount of every good has positive utility, the solution to the utility maximization problem entails consuming positive amounts of all goods: x∗ > 0. In this case, one can express the optimality conditions as

∇u( x∗ ) = λ p.

In weeks to follow we will see where these optimality conditions come from and what do they mean. For now, compute the gradient vector ∇u( x) for any x > 0. To do that, compute the partial derivative of u with respect to good xi and verify that it is a continuous function for all x > 0. Then you can write ! " ∂u( x) ∂u( x) ∇u( x) = ,..., . ∂x1 ∂xn 1 Most of the time we also impose the requirement that ∑i αi = 1, but this extra condition is not needed here.

Once you do that, get an expression for the marginal rate of substitution between goods i and j, i.e., the ratio of marginal utilities:

MUi ( x) ∂u( x)/∂xi MRSij ( x) = = . MUj ( x) ∂u( x)/∂x j

Problem 3. Let f : R2 → R be defined by

f ( x) = x12 x2 + x2 .

(a) Compute the partial derivatives of f . (b) Verify that both partial derivatives are continuous functions, and hence we can write ! " ⊤ ∂ f ( x) ∂ f ( x) D f ( x) = ∇ f ( x) = , . ∂x1 ∂x2

(c) Use the definition of the derivative to verify that D f ( x) in part (b) is indeed the derivative of f at x.

Problem 4. Consider the following quadratic function g : R2 → R defined by

g( x) = ( a1 x1 + a2 x2 )2 + (b1 x1 + b2 x2 ) + c,

where a1 , a2 , b1 , b2 and c are real numbers. (a) Compute the partial derivatives of g. (b) Verify that both partial derivatives are continuous functions, and hence we can write ! " ⊤ ∂g( x) ∂g( x) Dg( x) = ∇ g( x) = , . ∂x1 ∂x2

(c) Use the definition of the derivative to verify that Dg( x) in part (b) is indeed the derivative of g at x.

Problem 5. Consider the function f : R3++ → R defined by

f ( x) = x2 x3 + x1 ln( x2 /x3 ).

(a) Compute the partial derivative of f with respect to x2 and x3 . (b) Use the definition of the partial derivative to obtain ∂ f ( x)/∂x1 .

Now consider the function g : R3 → R defined by

g( x) = ( x1 x2 x3 )2 + x3 exp( x1 x22 ).

(c) Compute the partial derivative of g with respect to x1 and x2 . (d) Use the definition of the partial derivative to obtain ∂g( x)/∂x3 .
